primary_diagnosis.name: Aortic dissection, descending (Stanford Type B), with lower-limb malperfusion

pathophysiology_summary: A tear in the aortic intima allows pulsatile blood to enter and dissect along the media, creating a false lumen. In a descending (Stanford Type B) dissection the flap originates distal to the left subclavian artery. Extension of the false lumen and the mobile intimal flap can occlude aortic branch vessels, producing malperfusion syndromes — here the flap has compromised left lower-limb arterial supply (acute limb ischaemia: pale, cold, pulseless, painful, paraesthetic leg) and threatens the mesenteric and renal circulations (flank pain, nausea). The dominant driver of propagation is aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt), which is why severe hypertension is both the cause and the target of treatment: rapid, aggressive control of blood pressure AND heart rate (impulse) with intravenous agents halts flap extension. Untreated, the dissection propagates to cause bowel, renal or limb infarction, or ruptures into rupture/haemorrhagic shock and death.

differential.evidence_basis: Abrupt maximal-intensity tearing interscapular pain migrating caudally is the classic dissection history.
Sudden severe truncal pain with diaphoresis — but this pain is interscapular/back, migrating downwards rather than to jaw/arm, and is accompanied by limb ischaemia.
Interscapular back pain — but abrupt maximal onset, no trauma, and objective limb ischaemia make a benign MSK cause a dangerous anchor.
Flank pain and nausea — but the pain began interscapularly and there are pulse/neurological deficits defining a vascular event, not urolithiasis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening story alone the pattern (instantaneous maximal tearing back pain + a cold, weak leg) should place a life-threatening vascular catastrophe at the top and trigger emergency escalation — not a benign back-pain pathway.

differential.diagnosis: aortic dissection (descending/Type B) with limb malperfusion
differential.evidence_basis: Migrating tearing pain + acutely ischaemic left leg + severe uncontrolled hypertension with non-adherence is a coherent dissection-with-malperfusion picture.

differential.diagnosis: acute limb ischaemia from arterial embolism/thrombosis (non-dissection)
differential.evidence_basis: A cold pulseless leg alone could be embolic, but here it is explained by the propagating dissection flap and preceded by tearing back pain.
Must not be missed and can coexist if the dissection involves the coronary ostia, but the migratory back-to-abdomen pain and limb ischaemia point away from primary ACS.
Pain not reproduced by palpation or movement, no trauma, and there is objective limb ischaemia — a benign MSK cause is excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Anchoring on back pain or on the leg in isolation is the trap; the unifying diagnosis is a dissection propagating distally.

differential.diagnosis: aortic dissection, descending (Stanford Type B), with lower-limb malperfusion
differential.evidence_basis: CT angiography would confirm an intimal flap originating distal to the left subclavian artery with a false lumen compromising left lower-limb perfusion. Diagnosis cannot be confirmed remotely — it is an emergency-department/imaging diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive diagnosis requires CT angiography; the telehealth task is recognition and immediate escalation, not confirmation.

red_flags.description: Abrupt, maximal-intensity tearing/ripping interscapular pain that peaks instantly
red_flags.clinical_significance: Instantaneous maximal 'tearing' truncal pain is the hallmark of aortic dissection and demands emergency vascular assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Propagation of the dissection to rupture or further branch-vessel malperfusion, with high mortality; every hour of delay in Type A/B management worsens outcome.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Migratory pain reflects an extending dissection flap along the aorta.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing propagation into mesenteric/renal/iliac vessels causing bowel, renal or limb infarction.

red_flags.description: Acute unilateral limb ischaemia — pale, cold, weak, numb 'dead' left leg (malperfusion)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A new cold, pulseless, painful, paraesthetic limb indicates acute arterial malperfusion — a limb-threatening and life-threatening complication of the dissection.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Irreversible limb ischaemia and loss, and a marker of a propagating dissection threatening other organs.

red_flags.description: Severe uncontrolled hypertension with recent antihypertensive non-adherence
red_flags.clinical_significance: Uncontrolled hypertension is both the driver of dissection propagation and the treatment target; extreme BP in this context is an emergency, not a chronic finding.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued high aortic wall shear accelerates flap propagation and rupture risk.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling abrupt interscapular pain as back strain and triaging it as benign — the single most dangerous error here; benign back pain does not begin at maximal intensity, migrate, or produce a cold pulseless leg.
Working up the cold leg as an isolated embolus, or the flank pain as renal colic, and missing the unifying dissection that explains all of it.
Treating the presentation as myocardial infarction and giving antiplatelets/anticoagulation or thrombolysis — catastrophic in dissection, provoking fatal haemorrhage.
Lowering blood pressure with a pure vasodilator before beta-blockade causes reflex tachycardia that increases aortic wall shear (dP/dt) and can worsen propagation.

Attempting to manage or reassure at home; the diagnosis needs urgent CT angiography and intravenous impulse control that only the emergency pathway can provide — the correct telehealth act is recognition and immediate 000.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Objective, accurate blood pressure and any inter-arm/inter-limb BP differential
Presence and symmetry of femoral, popliteal and pedal pulses (pulse deficit)
Abdominal examination for flank tenderness / mesenteric or renal malperfusion
CT angiography showing the intimal flap distal to the left subclavian artery
Aortic diameter, dissection extent (Stanford/DeBakey classification) and rupture status
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance, state a suspected life-threatening vascular emergency, keep the patient lying flat and completely still, and do NOT give aspirin or any anticoagulant. Definitive care is urgent CT angiography and intravenous blood-pressure and heart-rate control in hospital.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The AI Doctor cannot and must not confirm aortic dissection remotely; the risk is UNDER-action — reassuring or triaging as back pain — not over-precision. Recognise the emergency pattern and escalate.
